mappings.rationaleAI: Defines biological life through homeostasis, metabolism, growth, reproduction, and response to stimuli (A.1), with classification spanning Archaea, Bacteria, and Eukaryota (A.2), and addresses the possibility of extraterrestrial life (B).

Defines the individual functional unit of life through autonomous reproduction, growth, and metabolism (A.1), with boundary cases complicating the concept at various levels of biotic organization (A.2).
The scientific study of life covering cell theory, heredity, evolution, metabolism, and homeostasis (A.1), spanning all levels of organization from molecules and cells to ecosystems (A.2).
Characterizes the four classes of biological macromolecules — proteins, nucleic acids, carbohydrates, and lipids — at the molecular level of biotic organization (A.2.a).
One of four major biomolecule families, polysaccharides function as polymers at the molecular level of biotic organization (A.2.a), with energy storage and structural roles.
Hormones as signaling molecules regulate physiology in response to stimuli (A.1.c) and mediate interaction with the environment through negative feedback homeostasis (A.1.e).
Metabolism as the totality of enzyme-catalyzed reactions sustaining life, divided into catabolism and anabolism (A.1.b), is a defining property of life.
DNA is a nucleic acid polymer encoding genetic information, a fundamental polymer at the molecular level of biotic organization (A.2.a).
Proteins are large biomolecular polymers with functional breadth spanning enzymes, structural scaffolds, and transport carriers at the molecular level (A.2.a).

The origin of life from non-living chemistry relates to the concept of life on Earth (A), and the RNA world hypothesis and prebiotic chemistry connect to the concept of extraterrestrial life and its chemistry (B.1).
Bacteria are single-celled prokaryotic organisms, fundamental to the cellular level of biotic organization (A.2.b), exhibiting procaryotic cell structure.
Physiology studies functions and mechanisms of living systems, relevant to properties of life including metabolism and homeostasis (A.1) and organ-level organization (A.2.c).
Ecology studies organism-environment relationships at population and community levels of biotic organization (A.2.d), including species interactions and ecosystem dynamics.
Ecosystems represent the community level of biotic organization (A.2.d), comprising organisms interacting with their abiotic environment through nutrient cycles and energy flows.
Archaea constitute a prokaryotic domain of life, relevant to the cellular level of biotic organization (A.2.b) with procaryotic cell characteristics distinct from bacteria.
Viruses as obligate intracellular agents challenge definitions of life properties (A.1), lacking autonomous metabolism yet evolving and replicating within host cells.
The cell is the fundamental unit of life, directly relevant to the cellular level of biotic organization (A.2.b) including the prokaryote-eukaryote distinction.
RNA as a nucleic acid polymer bridging genetic information and protein synthesis at the molecular level (A.2.a), with the RNA world hypothesis relevant to the origin of life.
Investigates molecular structures underlying biological activity at the molecular level of biotic organization (A.2.a), centered on nucleic acids and proteins.
The study of heredity relates to reproduction as a property of life (A.1.d) and nucleic acids at the molecular level (A.2.a).
Nutrition supplies nutrients for energy production and chemical synthesis relevant to metabolism (A.1.b) and organismal interaction with the environment (A.1.e).
Natural selection operates through interaction with the environment (A.1.e) and shifts trait frequencies within populations (A.2.d).
Taxonomy classifies organisms into hierarchical ranks, organizing the population level of biotic organization including species and communities (A.2.d).
Death as the irreversible cessation of biological functions represents the termination of life properties including metabolism, reproduction, and response to stimuli (A.1).
Bone as mineralized connective tissue functions at the organ level (A.2.c), serving as part of tissue systems within the whole organism.
Flowers are reproductive structures of angiosperms, directly relevant to reproduction as a property of life (A.1.d).
Reproduction as the biological process producing offspring is a defining property of life (A.1.d), encompassing asexual and sexual strategies.
Evolution changes heritable characteristics across generations through reproduction (A.1.d), operating at population and community levels (A.2.d).
Venus connects to astrobiology and the search for life beyond Earth (B), with active mission plans including Venus Life Finder relevant to the concept of extraterrestrial life (B.1).
Energy as the capacity to do work is central to metabolism (A.1.b), the biochemical processes sustaining life through catabolism and anabolism.
Photosynthesis converts light energy into chemical energy as a metabolic process (A.1.b), representing a fundamental mode of interaction with the environment (A.1.e).
